message.body: Your toll invoice is overdue. Pay within 30 minutes to avoid suspension: http://sunpass-billing-pay.com

explanation.short: Government toll services don't demand payment in 30 minutes via random links.
tells: Domain 'sunpass-billing-pay.com' doesn't match official sunpass.com
30-minute urgency is a classic pressure tactic
Real SunPass contacts you from a short code, not a random 10-digit number
Threats of immediate suspension are designed to bypass rational thinking
explanation.safe_move: Open sunpass.com directly in your browser. Check your balance there. Never click links in unsolicited texts.
explanation.consequence: This link leads to a credential-harvesting page mimicking SunPass. Entering your info hands the attacker your login and any saved payment method.

message.body: USPS: Your package 9400111899223767680091 is out for delivery today. Track at usps.com/track

explanation.short: This is a legitimate USPS tracking notification. The short code, official domain, and tracking format are all consistent with real USPS alerts.
tells: Short code 28777 is the official USPS notification number
Links only to usps.com — not a lookalike
No payment request, no urgency pressure
Tracking number format matches real USPS codes
explanation.safe_move: Safe to tap the link, or go to usps.com/track directly and enter the tracking number.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring legitimate shipping notifications can mean a missed delivery or unclaimed package.

message.body: FedEx: Your package has been held. Pay $2.99 redelivery fee to release it: fedex-redelivery-fee.net/pay

explanation.short: FedEx never texts you asking for redelivery fees via random links. This is a classic package-held scam.
tells: FedEx communicates via short codes, not random 10-digit numbers
FedEx never charges redelivery fees via text link
Domain uses .net and a hyphenated name — fedex.com is the only official domain
Small dollar amounts ($2.99) are designed to seem harmless but capture your card details
explanation.safe_move: Go to fedex.com directly and enter your tracking number. Call FedEx at 1-800-463-3339 if you have concerns.
explanation.consequence: Your payment card details go directly to scammers. The $2.99 is bait — they'll charge much more once they have your card.

message.body: CHASE ALERT: Suspicious activity detected. Your account will be locked in 24 hours. Verify now: chase-secure-verify.com

red_flags.label: Unknown phone number (not Chase short code)

explanation.short: Chase contacts customers via official short codes, not random numbers. The domain is a lookalike.
tells: Real Chase alerts come from short code 24273, not a 10-digit number
chase-secure-verify.com is not chase.com
Account lockout threats in 24 hours are pressure tactics
Chase's real fraud alerts ask you to call the number on your card — not click links
explanation.safe_move: Call the number on the back of your Chase card. Log in at chase.com directly (never via a text link).
explanation.consequence: The lookalike site captures your Chase username, password, and potentially your 2FA code, giving the attacker full account access.

message.body: Chase Fraud: Did you attempt a $432.19 purchase at BEST BUY on 2/26? Reply YES or NO. Do NOT click any links.

explanation.short: This is a real Chase fraud alert. Short codes, no links, reply-only — all consistent with legitimate bank fraud detection.
tells: Short code 24273 is Chase's official fraud alert number
Explicitly says 'Do NOT click any links'
Only asks YES/NO — no passwords, SSN, or account numbers
References specific merchant and amount (real alerts do this)
explanation.safe_move: Reply YES or NO. If in doubt, call the number on the back of your Chase card before replying.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring this delays fraud detection. A YES or NO helps Chase protect your account in real time.

message.subject: Action Required: Unusual Sign-In Activity Detected
message.body:
Dear Valued Customer,

We have detected unusual sign-in activity on your Wells Fargo account from a new device.

To protect your account, we have temporarily limited access. Please verify your identity within 48 hours to restore full access.

[Verify My Account]

If you do not verify, your account will be permanently suspended.

Wells Fargo Customer Security Team

explanation.short: Wells Fargo emails come from @wellsfargo.com. 'wellsfargo-alerts.com' is a scammer-owned lookalike domain.
tells: Official domain is wellsfargo.com — not wellsfargo-alerts.com
Real Wells Fargo emails use your actual name, not 'Valued Customer'
Permanent suspension threats are coercion — banks don't work this way
48-hour pressure windows are designed to short-circuit rational thinking
explanation.safe_move: Call Wells Fargo at 1-800-869-3557. Log into wellsfargo.com directly from your browser.
explanation.consequence: Clicking 'Verify My Account' leads to a phishing page that captures your username, password, and security questions — full account takeover.

message.subject: Your Apple ID has been locked
message.body:
Your Apple ID has been locked due to suspicious activity.

Click below to unlock your account and verify your identity. Failure to do so within 24 hours will result in permanent account deletion.

[Unlock Apple ID]

Apple Support

explanation.short: Apple only sends emails from @apple.com or @id.apple.com. 'apple-id-verify.com' is attacker-owned.
tells: apple-id-verify.com is not apple.com
Apple never threatens permanent deletion via email — they lock accounts temporarily
24-hour ultimatums are pressure tactics to bypass your skepticism
explanation.safe_move: Go to appleid.apple.com directly and check your account status. If locked, reset via Apple's official flow.
explanation.consequence: The phishing page captures your Apple ID and password. With that, the attacker accesses iCloud, Photos, contacts, and any apps signed in with Apple ID.

message.subject: New sign-in to your Google Account
message.body:
Hi Kevin,

Your Google Account was just signed in to from a new Windows device.

Time: Thursday, February 26, 2026, 3:42 PM EST
Location: New York, NY

If this was you, you don't need to do anything.

If you didn't sign in, go to your Google Account to secure it.

The Google Accounts Team

explanation.short: This is a real Google security notification. Sent from accounts.google.com, personalizes your name, provides specific time and location, and doesn't pressure you to click anything.
tells: accounts.google.com is Google's official authentication domain
Uses your actual name (not 'Valued User')
Includes specific device, time, and location information
Explicitly says 'if this was you, you don't need to do anything' — no pressure
explanation.safe_move: If it was you, no action needed. If it wasn't you, go to myaccount.google.com directly (not via the email link) and check recent activity.
explanation.consequence: Dismissing a real security alert when you didn't sign in means an attacker may have your account.

message.body: MICROSOFT ALERT: Virus detected on your device! Your data is at risk. Call us immediately: 1-888-555-0192 to remove the threat.

explanation.short: Microsoft does not send unsolicited texts about viruses. This is a tech support scam designed to get you to call a fake support line.
tells: Microsoft never contacts customers via random text messages
Legitimate antivirus alerts appear on your device screen — not via SMS
Calling the number connects you to scammers who will ask for remote access to your computer
"Immediate" call pressure is a hallmark of tech support fraud
explanation.safe_move: Delete the message. Run your device's built-in security scan if concerned. Call Microsoft directly at 1-800-642-7676 if you need support.
explanation.consequence: Calling the number connects you to scammers who guide you through granting them remote access to your computer, then steal files, install malware, and demand payment.

message.subject: Your Norton subscription has been renewed - $349.99 charged
message.body:
Dear Customer,

Thank you for your continued trust in Norton. Your subscription has been automatically renewed.

Amount charged: $349.99
Transaction ID: NRT-2026-8847291

If you did NOT authorize this charge, call our billing team within 24 hours to cancel and receive a full refund:

1-888-555-0247

Norton Billing Department

red_flags.label: Unexpected large charge you don't recognize

explanation.short: This is a fake refund scam. The charge never happened. Calling the number connects you to scammers who steal banking info while 'processing the refund.'
tells: norton-renewal-center.com is not norton.com
The charge doesn't appear on your actual credit card statement
Real Norton renewal emails link to your Norton account — they don't give you a phone number
24-hour pressure to call is designed to prevent you from checking your actual bank statement first
explanation.safe_move: Check your actual credit card statement — the charge won't be there. Check your Norton account at norton.com directly.
explanation.consequence: Calling the number leads to 'tech support' who ask to remotely access your computer to 'process the refund,' then install malware or directly transfer money from your bank.

message.subject: Exciting remote opportunity - $85/hr - immediate start
message.body:
Hi there,

I came across your profile and think you'd be a perfect fit for a remote Data Entry Specialist role.

Compensation: $85/hr, 20 hrs/week (work from home)
Start date: Immediately
Requirement: Must purchase a home office setup ($450) — fully reimbursed in first paycheck.

Interested? Reply with your full name, phone, address, and best time to call.

Best,
Sarah Chen
Talent Hire Network

red_flags.label: Pay seems too high for the role

explanation.short: Legitimate employers never ask you to pay for equipment upfront. This is a classic advance-fee job scam.
tells: No legitimate employer requires you to buy your own setup before starting
$85/hr for data entry is unrealistically high
Asking for home address in a cold recruiting email is a red flag
Vague company name, non-corporate email domain, no mention of company website
explanation.safe_move: Never pay for a job. Search the company name + 'scam' online. Verify the recruiter on LinkedIn using their actual LinkedIn URL, not info in the email.
explanation.consequence: You send $450 for 'equipment' that never arrives. The check they send you for 'reimbursement' bounces — after you've already transferred funds. You're out the full amount.

message.subject: Following up on your application - Software Engineer role at Stripe
message.body:
Hi Kevin,

Thanks for applying to the Software Engineer position at Stripe. I'm a recruiter on the engineering team here.

I'd love to set up a 30-minute intro call to tell you more about the role and learn about your background.

Are you available this week? You can book a time here: calendly.com/maya-stripe

Looking forward to connecting!

Maya Patel
Technical Recruiter, Stripe
stripe.com | LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/maya-patel-stripe

red_flags.label: Pay seems too high for the role

explanation.short: This is a legitimate recruiter follow-up. Sent from stripe.com, references your application, asks for a call with no strings attached, and includes verifiable contact details.
tells: Email from @stripe.com — the official company domain
References your actual application — you applied first
No requests for money, equipment purchases, or personal info
LinkedIn profile and Calendly links are verifiable
explanation.safe_move: Verify maya.patel on LinkedIn before the call. Calendly.com/maya-stripe is a legitimate scheduling link. Proceed with the call.
explanation.consequence: Missing a real recruiter follow-up could cost you a job opportunity.

message.subject: URGENT: Updated banking details for invoice #INV-2026-0892
message.body:
Hi,

Please note that our banking details have changed effective immediately. All outstanding payments must be directed to our new account.

Bank: First National Bank
Routing: 021000089
Account: 7734829104

This applies to invoice #INV-2026-0892 ($14,750) due this Friday.

Please confirm receipt of this email.

David Reyes
Accounts Receivable
Acme Corp

explanation.short: This is a Business Email Compromise (BEC) scam. Attackers impersonate vendors and redirect payments to their own accounts.
tells: acme-corp-invoicing.com is not acme.com — a lookalike domain set up by the attacker
Legitimate vendors almost never change banking details via a single email
You should always verify bank account changes by calling the vendor at a number you already have on file — not one in the email
explanation.safe_move: Call David Reyes directly using the phone number from your existing records (not from this email). Verify the change verbally before any payment.
explanation.consequence: $14,750 is wired to the attacker's account. Wire transfers are nearly impossible to reverse once sent. This is one of the most costly scam types for businesses.

message.subject: Invoice #1047 from Greenleaf Design Co. - $2,400.00 due Mar 1
message.body:
You have a new invoice from Greenleaf Design Co.

Invoice #1047

Due date: March 1, 2026
Description: Website redesign — Phase 2 completion

Pay securely at: qbo.intuit.com/pay/...

Questions? Contact Greenleaf Design at hello@greenleafdesign.com or (415) 555-0132.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate QuickBooks payment request. Sent from intuit.com, the payment link goes to qbo.intuit.com, and vendor contact details are verifiable.
tells: invoicing@quickbooks.intuit.com is Intuit's official invoicing domain
Payment link goes to qbo.intuit.com — Intuit's secure platform
Provides vendor's independent contact info to verify
References a real project you agreed to ('Website redesign — Phase 2')
explanation.safe_move: Verify the amount with Greenleaf Design directly if needed. Safe to pay via the QuickBooks link — it's Intuit's own infrastructure.
explanation.consequence: Delaying a legitimate vendor invoice can damage business relationships.

message.body: Hey! I know this is random but I saw your profile and couldn't scroll past 😊 I'm Emma — I'm actually a financial advisor and I've been doing really well in crypto lately. Would love to chat more and maybe share some tips? I could help you make some serious returns. No pressure! Just thought I might as well reach out ❤️

explanation.short: This is a pig-butchering scam opener. The 'financial advisor' narrative followed by crypto tips is a textbook setup for investment fraud.
tells: Unsolicited compliments + investment pitch in the same message is a pig-butchering scam pattern
Real financial advisors don't cold DM strangers with investment tips
Crypto investment platforms in these scams are fake — you see fake gains, but can never withdraw
explanation.safe_move: Don't reply. Block and report the account. Romantic interest + investment talk is almost always a pig-butchering scam.
explanation.consequence: Engagement leads to a months-long relationship where trust is built before you're asked to invest on a platform they control. Victims typically lose $5,000–$500,000+ before realizing the fraud.

message.body: Hey! We met at the ProductCon meetup last week — I was the one demoing the Figma plugin. Loved chatting about design systems. Would love to continue the conversation if you're open to it! Also happy to share the plugin link if you wanted to try it.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate follow-up from a real networking event. Specific context (ProductCon, Figma plugin, design systems) makes fabrication unlikely.
tells: References a specific shared experience — hard to fake without knowing you
No investment pitch, no urgency, no financial angle
The link offer is to a product, not a financial platform
Profile handle is consistent with the name — @sarahkim_ux not @emma_j_official123
explanation.safe_move: If you did attend ProductCon and match the context, safe to reply. Verify the Figma plugin link by searching for it independently rather than using her link.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring legitimate networking contacts unnecessarily limits professional opportunities.

message.body: ParkNow: Your parking session at Lot B has expired. Avoid a $75 fine — extend your session now: [QR Code image] or bit.ly/parknow-extend

explanation.short: Legitimate parking apps contact you through their own app, not random texts with shortlinks. This is a fake parking scam.
tells: Legitimate parking apps send notifications through the app, not random SMS
URL shorteners (bit.ly) hide the destination — red flag in payment contexts
The fine threat ($75) is designed to make the small payment feel worth avoiding
Real parking meters don't have your phone number unless you registered with an official app
explanation.safe_move: Open your parking app directly to check your session. Use only the app's built-in payment flow.
explanation.consequence: The link or QR leads to a payment page that captures your card details. The 'parking fine' is fake — the card charge is very real.

message.subject: Action Required: Multi-Factor Authentication Update
message.body:
Dear Employee,

Our systems are upgrading to a new MFA platform. All employees must re-register their authenticator app within 48 hours to maintain access.

Please scan the QR code below to complete registration:

[QR Code Image]

This QR will expire in 48 hours. Do not share this code with anyone.

IT Security Team

explanation.short: Real IT departments send MFA updates from your company's official domain, not external .net addresses. Scanning this QR could hand attackers your login.
tells: company-it-support.net is not your employer's domain — attackers register these to look credible
Real IT MFA changes come through official IT ticketing systems or company email
QR codes in phishing emails can initiate OAuth flows that grant attackers account access
48-hour deadline discourages verification through proper channels
explanation.safe_move: Contact your IT department directly through your internal directory (Slack, Teams, or the company phone number) — not by replying to this email.
explanation.consequence: Scanning the QR code triggers an OAuth authentication flow that grants the attacker full access to your work account — email, files, and internal systems.

message.subject: Your ticket for UX Summit 2026 - March 15
message.body:
Hi Kevin,

You're registered for UX Summit 2026!

Event: UX Summit 2026

Venue: Javits Center, New York

Your ticket QR code is attached. Show this at the door for entry.

Manage your registration at eventbrite.com/mytickets

explanation.short: This is a legitimate event ticket confirmation. Sent from eventbrite.com, the QR is your entry ticket (not an auth flow), and you registered for this event.
tells: order@eventbrite.com is Eventbrite's official transactional email domain
You initiated this — you registered for the event
QR code is for event entry, not authentication — no account access is granted
Links go to eventbrite.com/mytickets — Eventbrite's own platform
explanation.safe_move: Safe to save your ticket. Verify the event at eventbrite.com if uncertain.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a legitimate ticket email means you might not have your QR code ready at the door.

message.body: BofA: Did you send $987 via Zelle to Mike Johnson? Reply YES or NO. If NO, call 1-800-555-0192 immediately.

explanation.short: The question itself may be real (Bank of America does ask this), but the phone number is wrong. Always call the number on your card — never the number in a text.
tells: Bank of America's official fraud short code is 900-00 — not 692-6328
The text format is consistent with real fraud alerts, making this a harder call
The danger is the callback number: scammers intercept by providing their own 'bank' number
If you reply NO and call the number in the text, you reach a scammer posing as BofA
explanation.safe_move: Reply NO to the text. Then call the number on the BACK OF YOUR CARD — not the number provided. Never use a phone number given to you in a message you didn't initiate.
explanation.consequence: Calling the provided number connects you to a scammer posing as Bank of America. They walk you through 'securing' your account while actually transferring funds out.

message.subject: Your Apple ID was used to sign in on a new device
message.body:
Your Apple ID (kevin@example.com) was used to sign in to iCloud via a web browser.

Date and Time: February 26, 2026, 3:14 PM EST
Browser: Chrome on Windows

If you recently signed in, you can disregard this email.

If you didn't sign in, your account may be compromised. Go to your Apple ID account page at appleid.apple.com.

Apple Support

explanation.short: This is a legitimate Apple security notification. Apple sends these from email.apple.com, includes specific device and time details, and doesn't pressure you to click anything.
tells: email.apple.com is Apple's official transactional email subdomain
Includes your actual Apple ID email address
Provides specific browser, platform, time, and timezone
No pressure — says 'disregard if this was you'
explanation.safe_move: If the sign-in was you, no action needed. If not, go to appleid.apple.com directly — not via any link in the email.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a real Apple security alert when you didn't sign in means someone may have access to your iCloud, Photos, and connected apps.

message.body: Hello! We found your profile impressive. We have a remote work opportunity paying $50-80/hr — data review work, flexible hours, no experience needed. To apply, please send your name, address, SSN, and a photo of your ID to verify eligibility. Reply here to start immediately!

red_flags.label: Handle mimics real company (LinkedlN vs LinkedIn)

explanation.short: LinkedIn Jobs (@LinkedlnJobs) has a lowercase 'L' instead of uppercase 'I' — classic impersonation. No legitimate employer asks for SSN via DM before an interview.
tells: Handle is @LinkedlnJobs (ln) not @LinkedInJobs (In) — a lookalike username
SSN + photo ID in a DM before any interview is immediate identity theft
$50-80/hr for 'data review, no experience needed' is unrealistically high
LinkedIn's own job notifications don't work this way
explanation.safe_move: Never provide SSN or government ID via social media DMs. Report the account. Verify jobs at linkedin.com directly.
explanation.consequence: Your SSN and photo ID enable full identity theft: new credit accounts, tax fraud, and synthetic identity fraud in your name.

message.body: UPS: We attempted delivery but nobody was home. Pay a $3.50 rescheduling fee to release your package: ups-reschedule-delivery.com/pay

explanation.short: UPS does not charge rescheduling fees via text. Small fees are bait to capture your card details.
tells: UPS uses short code 69877, not a 10-digit number
ups-reschedule-delivery.com is not ups.com
UPS never charges redelivery or rescheduling fees
Tiny dollar amounts ($3.50) are designed to seem harmless but capture your card
explanation.safe_move: Go to ups.com directly and enter your tracking number. Call UPS at 1-800-742-5877 if you have questions.
explanation.consequence: Entering card details on the fake site gives scammers your full card number and CVV. They charge far larger amounts once they confirm the card is active.

message.subject: Your package has shipped — arriving tomorrow
message.body:
Hello Kevin,

Your order has shipped and is on its way!

Estimated delivery: Thursday, February 27

tells: shipment-tracking@amazon.com is Amazon's official transactional email domain
Order ID format matches real Amazon order numbers
UPS tracking number format (1Z...) is standard and verifiable
No payment request, no urgency, no threats
explanation.safe_move: Safe to track at amazon.com/orders. Enter the tracking number on ups.com directly if you prefer.

message.subject: Customs Hold: Action Required for Package CW8847291
message.body:
Dear Recipient,

Your international shipment (Tracking: CW8847291) is being held by customs. A customs clearance fee of $19.99 must be paid within 72 hours to release your package.

Failure to pay will result in the package being returned to sender or destroyed.

[Pay Customs Fee Now]

DHL Customs Services

explanation.short: DHL's official domain is dhl.com. dhl-customs-clearance.com is attacker-owned. Real customs fees are handled through official government customs agencies, not email links.
tells: dhl-customs-clearance.com is not dhl.com — a scammer-registered lookalike
Real customs fees are assessed by CBP (U.S. Customs), not the shipping carrier via email
DHL emails address you by name, not 'Dear Recipient'
72-hour deadlines with destruction threats are pressure tactics
explanation.safe_move: Go to dhl.com and enter your tracking number to see the real status. Contact DHL directly at 1-800-225-5345.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the payment link leads to a page capturing your card and personal details. The 'customs fee' is fabricated — no such hold exists.

message.body: UPS: Your pkg 1Z999AA10123456784 is out for delivery today by 7pm. No action needed. Track: ups.com/track

explanation.short: This is a real UPS delivery notification. Short code 69877 is UPS's official SMS number, the link goes to ups.com, and no payment or action is requested.
tells: 69877 is UPS's official short code for delivery alerts
Link goes to ups.com — the official domain
Tracking number format (1Z...) is standard UPS format
'No action needed' — no payment, no click required
explanation.safe_move: Safe to track on ups.com/track. No action needed unless you want to redirect delivery.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a real delivery notification may result in a missed delivery or package held at a facility.

message.body: E-ZPass NJ: Your account balance is low and a toll transaction of $2.15 on 2/25 was not collected. Add funds within 48 hours to avoid a $50 penalty: ezpass-nj-account.com

explanation.short: E-ZPass NJ communicates via official short codes, not random numbers. ezpass-nj-account.com is not the official domain (ezpassnj.com).
tells: E-ZPass NJ uses short codes and the MyAccount portal — not random 10-digit numbers
ezpass-nj-account.com is not ezpassnj.com or e-zpassny.com
Disproportionate penalty ($50 for $2.15 toll) is designed to trigger urgency
Real E-ZPass low-balance alerts go through the registered app or portal
explanation.safe_move: Log into your E-ZPass account directly at ezpassnj.com or the official app. Verify any balance issues there.
explanation.consequence: The fake site captures your E-ZPass credentials and payment card. Attackers drain the linked card and may lock you out of your account.

message.body: FedEx: Package delivered to front door at 2:14 PM. Tracking: 789120987654321. Questions? fedex.com/track

explanation.short: This is a real FedEx delivery confirmation. Sent from the FedEx sender ID, links to fedex.com, no payment requested.
tells: Sender ID 'FedEx' is FedEx's registered alphanumeric sender
Link goes to fedex.com — the official domain
Delivery confirmation with specific time and location is standard FedEx format
No payment request, no urgency, no threatening language
explanation.safe_move: Safe. Your package is delivered. Visit fedex.com/track to confirm details if needed.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this message. You can ignore it once you've confirmed your package arrived.

message.subject: Your mail is being held — confirm your address
message.body:
Dear Customer,

USPS attempted to deliver a piece of certified mail to your address but was unable to complete delivery. To reschedule delivery and confirm your address, please verify your information at the link below.

[Confirm Delivery Address]

Note: Unconfirmed mail will be returned to sender after 10 days.

USPS Mail Services

red_flags.label: Asks you to confirm personal information
No tracking number or specific mail details

explanation.short: USPS sends email notifications from usps.com, not usps-mail-center.com. The vague reference and address confirmation request are phishing hallmarks.
tells: USPS official email domain is usps.com — not usps-mail-center.com
No tracking number provided — real USPS missed-delivery notices always include one
USPS never asks you to 'confirm your address' via an email link
Real USPS notices are left physically at the delivery address, not emailed
explanation.safe_move: Check your physical mailbox for a USPS Form 3849 notice. Track any expected packages at usps.com/track directly.
explanation.consequence: The 'confirm address' link collects your name, address, and potentially payment details for 'redelivery fees.' Your data is sold or used for identity fraud.

message.subject: New sign-in to your Capital One account
message.body:
Hi Kevin,

We noticed a new sign-in to your Capital One account.

When: February 26, 2026 at 4:11 PM ET
Device: iPhone
Location: New York, NY

If this was you, no action is needed.

If you don't recognize this activity, please call the number on the back of your card or visit capitalone.com immediately.

Capital One

explanation.short: This is a real Capital One security notification. Sent from capitalone.com, personalized with your name, includes specific device/location, and instructs you to call the back-of-card number — not a link.
tells: capitalone.com is Capital One's official domain
Uses your first name — not 'Dear Customer'
Includes specific device type and location information
Directs you to call the card's back number — the safe response to fraud
explanation.safe_move: If you signed in, no action needed. If not, call the number on the back of your Capital One card immediately.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring this alert when you didn't sign in means an attacker may have your account.

message.body: Venmo: Suspicious login detected on your account. To secure your account, verify now: venmo-account-secure.com/verify

explanation.short: Venmo's official SMS short code is 86753. Short code 900-09 is not Venmo. The link goes to a lookalike domain, not venmo.com.
tells: Venmo's real short code is 86753 — 900-09 is not affiliated with Venmo
venmo-account-secure.com is not venmo.com
Real Venmo security alerts link to venmo.com and never use hyphenated domains
The message is vague — no transaction details, no specific device mentioned
explanation.safe_move: Open the Venmo app directly and check for any security notifications there. Call Venmo support at 1-855-812-4430 if concerned.
explanation.consequence: The link harvests your Venmo login credentials. With access, attackers drain your balance or linked bank account via immediate transfers.

message.subject: Your PayPal account has been limited
message.body:
Dear Customer,

Your PayPal account access has been limited due to unusual activity. To restore full access, you must verify your identity within 48 hours.

[Restore My Account]

If you do not complete verification, your account will be permanently suspended and any funds will be held for 180 days.

PayPal Account Services

explanation.short: PayPal only emails from @paypal.com. paypal-account-alert.com is a scammer-owned lookalike domain.
tells: PayPal's official email domain is @paypal.com — not paypal-account-alert.com

180-day fund-hold threat is fabricated — PayPal doesn't operate this way
Real PayPal limitation notices link only to paypal.com and include a case ID
explanation.safe_move: Log into paypal.com directly (not via any link) and check the Resolution Center for any real limitations.
explanation.consequence: The link leads to a PayPal login clone. Entering credentials gives attackers full account access to drain your balance and linked bank accounts.

tells: 93557 is Wells Fargo's registered OTP short code
OTP messages are initiated by your own login attempt — you requested this
Explicitly says 'don't share with anyone, including Wells Fargo employees' — a legitimate security warning
No links, no payment requests, no urgency beyond the 10-minute window
explanation.safe_move: Enter the code on the Wells Fargo page you're already on. Never read this code aloud to anyone who calls you.
explanation.consequence: If someone calls you and asks for this code, they're a scammer using social engineering to bypass 2FA and take over your account.

Real Citi emails use your full name — not 'Valued Citi Customer'

explanation.safe_move: Log into citibank.com directly from your browser. If a real update is needed, it will be shown after login.
explanation.consequence: Clicking 'Update My Citi Account' takes you to a credential-harvesting page styled like Citi's real login portal.

tells: Apple Pay alerts appear in the Wallet app and on your lock screen — never via SMS from a 10-digit number
Calling the provided number connects you to a fake Apple support center
TECH-STORE-NYC is a vague merchant name designed to seem real
Apple's real support number is 1-800-275-2273 — always verify before calling
explanation.safe_move: Check your Wallet app directly for any transaction history. If concerned, call Apple Support at 1-800-275-2273.
explanation.consequence: Calling the number connects you to scammers who ask for your Apple ID and payment card details to 'reverse the transaction' — then use them to make real charges.

The mailed check will be fraudulent — it clears initially then bounces days later

explanation.safe_move: Delete and report. Legitimate mystery shopping opportunities are found through MSPA-verified companies — never via cold email.
explanation.consequence: You buy $200 in gift cards and share the codes. The reimbursement check bounces a week later. You're out $200 and your bank may charge you a returned check fee.

message.body: Hey! I noticed you follow finance accounts and wanted to reach out. I'm a crypto trader and I'm looking to bring on a few part-time 'trade assistants' — you'd help execute trades using our platform and keep 20% of the profits. No experience needed, I'll train you. Minimum $500 starting deposit on our platform to begin. Interested?

tells: Legitimate jobs never require an upfront deposit to start working

The 20% profit promise is bait — once you deposit, the platform goes silent or demands more
This is a pig-butchering variant using a job framing instead of romance
explanation.safe_move: Never deposit money to start a job. Block and report the account.
explanation.consequence: The $500 'deposit' goes directly to the scammer. The platform will show fake profits to encourage more deposits before ultimately disappearing with everything you've sent.

message.body:
Hi Kevin,

Our payroll provider is requiring updated tax withholding information for all employees before the March 1st deadline.

Please complete your W-2 update using the secure form below:

[Update W-2 Information]

This must be completed by March 1st to ensure your paycheck is not delayed.

Jennifer Walsh
HR & Payroll
yourcompany.com

explanation.short: HR and payroll updates are handled through official internal systems — never via links in external emails. yourcompany-hr.com is not your employer's domain.
tells: yourcompany-hr.com is not your employer's actual domain
Payroll providers update W-2 info through the official HR portal (ADP, Workday, etc.) — never a link in an email
Real HR communications about payroll come from @yourcompany.com accounts
Paycheck delay threats create pressure to act before verifying
explanation.safe_move: Contact HR directly using the phone number or email from your company directory. Log into your official HR portal (Workday, ADP, etc.) to update any tax information.
explanation.consequence: W-2 information can be used to file fraudulent tax returns in your name, claiming your refund before you file. Tax identity theft can take 12–18 months to resolve.

message.subject: Confidential — wire transfer needed today
message.body:
Kevin,

I need you to process an urgent wire transfer today for a confidential acquisition deal we're closing. I'm in meetings and can't discuss over the phone right now.

Amount: $47,500
Bank: First National Bank
Routing: 021000021
Account: 5567341029
Memo: Consulting Services

Please process this immediately and confirm via email. Do not discuss with others — this is confidential and time-sensitive.

Thanks,
Michael

red_flags.label: Email from similar but different domain
Told not to discuss with others

explanation.short: This is a CEO fraud / Business Email Compromise (BEC) attack. The domain yourcompany.co mimics yourcompany.com. No legitimate executive requests wire transfers via email with a secrecy demand.
tells: yourcompany.co is not yourcompany.com — attackers register lookalike TLDs (.co vs .com)
Any secrecy demand ('don't discuss with others') is a major red flag — designed to prevent verification
Legitimate executives never request wire transfers via email alone — always verify verbally
Real acquisition deals have legal and finance teams involved — not solo email instructions
explanation.safe_move: Call the CEO directly using the number you have on file — not from this email. Verify any wire transfer request in person or by known phone before acting.
explanation.consequence: $47,500 is wired to an attacker's account. Wire transfers are nearly impossible to reverse. This scam costs businesses over $2 billion annually.

message.subject: Copyright Infringement Notice — Action Required Within 72 Hours
message.body:
Dear Website Owner,

This notice is to inform you that copyrighted material owned by our client has been identified on your website or social media profile.

Violation Case: DMCA-2026-78812
Infringement Type: Unauthorized image use

To avoid legal proceedings and statutory damages of up to $150,000, you must either:
1. Remove the content immediately, OR
2. Pay a licensing settlement fee of $650 at the link below.

[Resolve This Claim]

Failure to respond within 72 hours will result in legal escalation.

DMCA Enforcement Office

red_flags.label: No specific content or URL identified

explanation.short: Legitimate DMCA notices name specific URLs and content. This notice is vague and demands payment via a non-official website — a hallmark of copyright extortion scams.
tells: dmca-copyright-enforcement.com is not an official DMCA authority — DMCA notices come from actual copyright holders
No specific content, URL, or image is identified — real DMCA notices are precise
Legitimate copyright holders pursue takedowns through official channels, not settlement payment links
$150,000 statutory damages apply only after federal court proceedings — not via an email link
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the payment link. If there's a real concern, consult an attorney or search the DMCA database at dmca.com. Report the extortion attempt to the FTC.
explanation.consequence: Paying the $650 confirms you as a target. Scammers will return with larger demands, knowing you'll pay to avoid perceived legal trouble.

message.subject: RE: Overpayment on Invoice #HR-2026-0441 — Return Required
message.body:
Hi Kevin,

We recently processed a payment for your consulting invoice but accidentally overpaid by $2,300.

We have already issued a corrected check. Please deposit the check you receive, deduct your original invoice amount, and return the $2,300 difference via Zelle to: payments@hr-solutions-staffing.net

We apologize for the error. Please process the return within 5 business days.

HR Solutions Payroll Team

red_flags.label: Asks you to return money via Zelle

explanation.short: This is the classic overpayment scam. The check they send is fraudulent. Once deposited, your bank holds you liable — and the 'return' you send via Zelle is real money going to scammers.
tells: hr-solutions-staffing.net does not match a company you have an existing relationship with
The check will be counterfeit — it clears initially but reverses in 5–10 days, after you've already sent $2,300 via Zelle
Legitimate companies correct overpayments through accounting, not Zelle transfers
No specific invoice number, project, or contract details are referenced
explanation.safe_move: Do not deposit any check from unknown parties. Contact your actual client via their known contact information to verify. Report to the FTC.
explanation.consequence: You deposit a fake check, send $2,300 via Zelle (irreversible), and the check bounces. You're out $2,300 and may owe your bank a returned check fee.

message.subject: Please DocuSign: Service Agreement — Greenfield Marketing LLC
message.body:

red_flags.label: Email not from main docusign.com domain
Contains a link to sign document

explanation.short: dse@docusign.net is DocuSign's official notification sender. This is a legitimate contract signing request — but you should verify the sender (Lisa Chen) before signing.
tells: dse@docusign.net is DocuSign's registered email domain for notifications (distinct from docusign.com by design)
Links to docusign.com — the official signing platform
Includes the actual sender's name and verifiable corporate email
You can contact Lisa Chen at greenfield-marketing.com to confirm she sent this before signing
explanation.safe_move: Before signing, verify with Lisa Chen via a separate channel (phone or email to lisa.chen@greenfield-marketing.com) that she sent this document. Then sign at docusign.com.
explanation.consequence: Signing a legitimate contract without reading it may bind you to terms you didn't intend. Always read what you're signing — even real documents.

message.body: Hello, I apologize for reaching out unexpectedly. I'm Colonel James Harrington, currently deployed in Syria. I came across your profile and found you to be a person of great character. I've been through a painful divorce and am looking for genuine connection. I have significant assets I need help transferring out of the region. I hope we might get to know each other better. God bless.

explanation.short: Military romance scams follow a well-documented pattern: deployed officer, painful backstory, rapid emotional connection, and eventually a financial request or 'asset transfer.'
tells: Deployed military officers cannot be reached by strangers on social media — accounts are either unofficial or fake
'Significant assets to transfer' is an advance-fee setup — you'll eventually be asked to help move money
Profile pictures of military officers are often stolen from real soldiers' public photos
The combination of loneliness appeal + financial angle is a classic long con opener
explanation.safe_move: Reverse image search the profile photo using Google Images or TinEye. Block and report the account. Do not engage further.
explanation.consequence: After weeks or months of emotional investment, the 'colonel' will need emergency money — medical bills, a flight home, a legal fee. Victims lose an average of $10,000+ before realizing the fraud.

message.body: Hey! I know you were a bit skeptical when we first talked but I really think you should try the platform I mentioned. I've been withdrawing profits every week and my account is at $34,000 now. I only started with $1,000 six months ago. I just want to share this with someone I trust. The minimum to start is $500. I can walk you through setup — it's really easy. Here's the link: crypto-wealth-platform.io/join?ref=emma

explanation.short: This is a pig-butchering follow-up. The platform is controlled by scammers. You'll see fake gains, but withdrawals will always be blocked by 'fees' or 'taxes' that must be paid upfront.
tells: 34x returns in 6 months ($1K to $34K) are fabricated — the platform shows fake balances
Referral links benefit the scammer — they earn a commission on your deposit
Attempts to withdraw will trigger additional 'tax' or 'verification' fees
The emotional relationship is engineered — you're being groomed to trust the financial recommendation
explanation.safe_move: Do not deposit any money. Block the account. Report to the FTC and the FBI's IC3 (ic3.gov).
explanation.consequence: Once you deposit, the platform shows impressive fake gains to encourage more investment. Withdrawal requests trigger new fees. Eventually the platform disappears with your money.

message.body: Hey Kevin! I watched your talk on API design at API World last October — really thought-provoking stuff on versioning strategy. I'm a backend engineer working on similar problems and would love to compare notes. Would you be up for a 30-min call sometime? No agenda other than swapping ideas.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate professional networking message. References a specific public event (API World) and specific topic from your talk — hard to fabricate without actually watching it.
tells: References a specific conference and specific topic — indicates genuine familiarity with your work
No investment pitch, no financial angle, no urgency
Simply asks for a peer conversation — no strings attached
Handle @davidpark_dev is consistent with a developer identity
explanation.safe_move: Verify @davidpark_dev on LinkedIn before agreeing to a call. A 30-minute peer conversation from a real attendee is a normal professional interaction.
explanation.consequence: Declining all professional outreach unnecessarily limits your network. Verify the person, then decide.

message.body: CONGRATULATIONS! Your mobile number was randomly selected in our international lottery sweep. You have won $750,000 USD. To claim your prize, please send a processing fee of $250 via wire transfer and provide your full name, address, and bank details to begin the transfer. This offer expires in 72 hours.

explanation.short: You cannot win a lottery you didn't enter. Any prize that requires an upfront fee is a scam — by definition, winning means you receive money, not pay it.
tells: No legitimate lottery selects winners who didn't purchase a ticket or enter
Requiring a fee to collect winnings is the defining characteristic of advance-fee fraud
Asking for bank account details before sending any money is how identity theft and account takeover begin
72-hour expiry is a pressure tactic to prevent you from thinking clearly
explanation.safe_move: Delete and block. This is one of the oldest and most common scam formats. Report to ic3.gov.
explanation.consequence: You pay $250, then face additional 'taxes' or 'legal fees' demanded before the prize can be sent. The prize never arrives. Bank details provided may be used to drain your account.

message.body: Dear Friend, I am Barrister Emmanuel Adeyemi, a legal practitioner in Lagos, Nigeria. My late client, who shares your surname, passed without a next of kin. His estate amounts to $4.7 million USD. With your assistance in claiming these funds as his relative, you will receive 40% ($1,880,000). This is completely legal and secure. Please reply with your full name and contact details to proceed.

explanation.short: This is the classic 'Nigerian prince' advance-fee fraud. After initial contact, you'll be asked to pay legal fees, taxes, or bribes before the 'inheritance' can be released — which never happens.
tells: Legitimate inheritance claims go through official legal channels — not social media DMs
Estates without next of kin go to the government in the jurisdiction of death — not to online strangers
The 40% share for 'doing nothing' creates greed that overrides skepticism — a deliberate manipulation
Providing your personal details begins a relationship that will eventually demand wire transfers
explanation.safe_move: Delete and block. This format has been in use since the 1980s (originally by fax). Report to ic3.gov.
explanation.consequence: After initial contact, you'll be asked to pay escalating 'legal fees' and 'release taxes' — thousands of dollars — before the inheritance can be transferred. It never is.

message.body: Hey Kevin! It's Marcus — we went to NYU together, I was in your Intro to CS class with Prof. Hernandez. I lost your number after changing phones a few years ago. Found you on here. I'm in NYC next week — would love to grab coffee and catch up if you're around?

explanation.short: This is a plausible legitimate reconnection. The message references specific shared context (NYU, Prof. Hernandez, Intro to CS) and makes a low-stakes request (coffee).
tells: References specific, verifiable shared context — class, professor name, institution
No financial angle, no urgency, no requests for personal information
A coffee meeting in public is a low-risk interaction to verify identity in person
Handle @marcuswebb83 with a birth year suggests a real personal account
explanation.safe_move: If the context rings true, safe to reply. You can verify shared memories in conversation before agreeing to meet. Meet in a public place.
explanation.consequence: Blocking all reconnection attempts from real people can unnecessarily sever genuine relationships.

message.body: I'm so sorry to bother you. I'm a single mom and my daughter was just diagnosed with leukemia. We are uninsured and the hospital is demanding $800 for her first treatment or they won't proceed. I'm reaching out to kind strangers because I have nowhere left to turn. Any amount would help. Please, her life depends on it. CashApp: $sarahmilligan24

explanation.short: Medical emergency appeals from strangers on social media are a common scam. Hospitals in the US are legally required to provide emergency care regardless of payment.
tells: EMTALA requires all US hospitals with emergency departments to provide stabilizing care regardless of ability to pay
Legitimate medical crowdfunding goes through verified platforms like GoFundMe — not stranger DMs
Unsolicited DMs asking for money from strangers, no matter how emotional, are nearly always fraudulent
Account handle with a recent year (@sarah_milligan_2024) suggests a freshly created account
explanation.safe_move: Do not send money. If you want to help with medical causes, donate to established charities like St. Jude's or through verified GoFundMe campaigns with documentation.
explanation.consequence: Money sent via CashApp to strangers is not recoverable. This is a common script used by fraud rings — your payment goes to a professional scammer, not a sick child.

message.body: USPS: Delivery failed — address issue. Scan the QR code to update your delivery address and reschedule: [QR Code] or usps-redelivery-update.com

explanation.short: USPS uses short code 28777 for SMS notifications — not 10-digit numbers. usps-redelivery-update.com is a phishing domain designed to collect your address and payment details.
tells: USPS official SMS short code is 28777 — not a 10-digit number
usps-redelivery-update.com is not usps.com
Real USPS delivery failures result in Form 3849 left at the door — not a QR code by text
Updating a delivery address via a texted QR code is not how USPS operates
explanation.safe_move: Visit usps.com directly and enter your tracking number. Call USPS at 1-800-275-8777 for delivery issues.
explanation.consequence: The QR or link leads to a fake USPS portal that collects your name, address, and a redelivery 'fee' — your card details and home address go to scammers.

message.subject: COVID-19 Test Result Verification — Action Required
message.body:
Dear New York Resident,

The NYC Department of Health requires all residents to verify their COVID-19 vaccination or test records in our updated database.

Scan the QR code below to access the verification portal and upload your documentation.

[QR Code Image]

Failure to complete by March 5, 2026 may affect your access to city services.

NYC Health Dept.

explanation.short: NYC government emails come from @health.nyc.gov — not nyc-health-verification.org. No city health department requires QR-based document uploads via unsolicited email.
tells: NYC government domains end in .nyc.gov — not .org domains registered by third parties
No current NYC requirement mandates vaccine or test verification for city services
Government health communications go through official portals, not unsolicited email QR codes
The vague threat about 'city services' is designed to provoke compliance without verification
explanation.safe_move: Contact the NYC Health Department directly via nyc.gov/health or call 311 to verify any real requirement.
explanation.consequence: Scanning the QR code takes you to a fake government portal that collects your personal information and potentially payment details for 'verification fees.'

message.body: Hello! Your account has been selected for Instagram's new Verified Badge program. To complete verification, scan the QR code below within 24 hours. This is a limited program and spots are filling fast. [QR Code]

explanation.short: Instagram's real verification handle is @instagram (verified with a blue checkmark). @instagram.support.team is an impersonator account. Scanning the QR initiates an account takeover OAuth flow.
tells: Instagram's official account is @instagram — not @instagram.support.team
Instagram verification requests are managed at instagram.com/accounts/request_verification — not via DMs
QR codes in this context trigger OAuth authorization flows that grant account access to attackers
Urgency + limited spots are pressure tactics to prevent you from verifying the sender
explanation.safe_move: Apply for verification through instagram.com/accounts/request_verification directly. Never scan QR codes sent via unsolicited DMs.
explanation.consequence: Scanning the QR code logs you into an attacker-controlled app with full access to your Instagram account — posts, DMs, and follower base.

Includes a real reservation number (MRR-4421897) matching your booking

explanation.consequence: No risk from this notification. Ignoring it just means you check in at the front desk instead.
